title: Scrapie: notifiable disease obligations for small flock keepers
subtitle: What to recognise, what to report, and why it matters.
excerpt: Scrapie is a fatal prion disease of sheep and goats. It has been present in the UK for centuries and cannot be treated. A smallholder with a confirmed case faces compulsory slaughter of affected animals and restrictions on flock movement. Knowing the signs and your reporting obligations avoids delays that worsen outcomes.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, gov.uk, scrapie notifiable disease guidance and reporting obligations. APHA scrapie information, apha.gov.uk. National Scrapie Plan for Great Britain annual reports.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of fatal neurodegenerative diseases caused by misfolded proteins (prions) rather than a virus or bacterium. In sheep and goats, the relevant disease is scrapie; in cattle, BSE. Prions are not destroyed by normal cooking, sterilising, or chemical treatments.

content.text: of sheep and goats with an incubation period of 2 to 5 years and a clinical course of weeks to months once signs appear. Affected animals deteriorate progressively and do not recover. It is a notifiable disease in the UK, meaning you are legally required to report suspected cases to the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA).
Rubbing and scratching against fences, posts, and other fixed surfaces, often the first observed sign
Progressive neurological deterioration: stumbling, head tremors, lip-smacking, abnormal posture
Loss of condition despite adequate food access
Exaggerated response to handling or sudden stimuli (hyperaesthesia)

If you observe any combination of the above signs in sheep or goats over 18 months of age, contact your vet immediately. Your vet must notify APHA if scrapie is clinically suspected; you as the keeper have a direct legal obligation to report any animal you reasonably suspect is affected. The APHA report line is 03000 200 301. Do not move animals off the holding pending investigation.

APHA will visit and take brain and lymph node samples from a dead or euthanised animal for testing. If scrapie is confirmed, compulsory measures follow under TSE law. These include slaughter of the affected cohort and breeding restrictions on the flock. How long the restrictions last depends on the genetic profile of your animals.

The National Scrapie Plan genotyped UK flocks for resistance alleles at the PrP gene. Sheep with the ARR/ARR genotype are the most resistant to classical scrapie. When buying breeding stock, ask about genotype if the seller participates in the NSP scheme. Buying from flocks with an unknown disease history or no NSP involvement carries a higher introduction risk.
